answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: "FLETCHER BUILDING'S THREE CLIMATE SCENARIOS OVERVIEW: 'Orderly' scenario (1.5°C) ... 'Disorderly' scenario (<2°C) ... 'Hot House World' scenario (>3°C)"
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, p.13–14; Appendix C, p.70–72
answers.notes: These are the three NZGBC 'Climate Scenarios for the Construction and Property Sector' adopted by Fletcher Building in FY24 and retained unchanged in FY25. For physical risk modelling (Appendix E), Marsh Advisory used SSP1–2.6 (analogue), SSP2–4.5, and SSP5–8.5 as the underlying model scenarios.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes by 2100 are described: 1.5°C (Orderly), below 2°C (Disorderly), and more than 3°C (Hot House World). No temperature overshoot is explicitly mentioned.
answers.evidence: "'Orderly' scenario (1.5°C) ... 'Disorderly' scenario (<2°C) Significant decarbonisation is delayed until 2030, leading to global warming being limited by 2100. ... 'Hot House World' scenario (>3°C) Global warming reaches more than 3 degrees above pre-industrial levels by 2100."
answers.notes: The Orderly scenario also notes the 'Orderly' scenario is arguably no longer considered reasonable, given that achieving a below-1.5°C pathway is increasingly viewed as unlikely, but it is maintained for comparability.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: The scenario provider is explicitly named as the New Zealand Green Building Council (NZGBC). Data inputs are sourced from NIWA (downscaled projections), IPCC, NGFS/IIASA, Ministry for the Environment (MfE), and the Climate Change Commission (CCC).
answers.evidence: "In FY24, Fletcher Building assessed three climate scenarios based on the New Zealand Green Building Council (NZGBC) 'Climate Scenarios for the Construction and Property Sector'. These were developed with participation from a wide range of industry and public sector stakeholders, and Fletcher Building contributed through representation on the NZGBC Leadership Group and Technical Working Group."
answers.notes: NIWA is named as the source of downscaled projections used within the NZGBC scenarios. Marsh Advisory conducted the FY25 physical risk modelling using RMS Climate on Demand.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: "The data for this scenario is sourced from downscaled NIWA projections from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Fifth Assessment Report (IPCC AR5) and is aligned to Representative Concentration Pathways (RCP) 2.6. Once downscaled data for IPCC AR6 is available, this scenario will align to Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP) 1-1.9. ... 'Disorderly' scenario ... is sourced from downscaled NIWA projections from IPCC AR5 and is aligned to RCP2.6. Once downscaled data for IPCC AR6 is available, this scenario will align to SSP1-2.6. ... 'Hot House World' scenario ... align to RCP 8.5 and SSP3."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, p.13; Appendix D, p.73
answers.notes: The document explicitly uses both RCP identifiers (RCP2.6, RCP8.5) and SSP identifiers (SSP1-1.9, SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0, SSP3) across the same scenarios, drawing on IPCC AR5 (RCP-based) and AR6 (SSP-based) data. The Appendix D data table also shows SSP1-1.9, SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0 for temperature data alongside RCP2.6 and RCP8.5 downscaling for other variables.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: "The data for this scenario is sourced from downscaled NIWA projections from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Fifth Assessment Report (IPCC AR5) ... In FY24, Fletcher Building assessed three climate scenarios based on the New Zealand Green Building Council (NZGBC) 'Climate Scenarios for the Construction and Property Sector'. These were developed with participation from a wide range of industry and public sector stakeholders ... From 2023 up to date: Conducted further scenario analysis, leveraging developed scenarios specific to its operations."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, p.13; Appendix D, p.73
answers.notes: The NZGBC scenarios are specifically tailored for the New Zealand construction and property sector. Physical data is downscaled by NIWA for New Zealand conditions. The scenarios incorporate NZ-specific policy, regulatory, and market assumptions. Marsh Advisory also applied climate analogues to adapt the modelling where preferred model runs were unavailable.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: "In FY24, Fletcher Building assessed three climate scenarios based on the New Zealand Green Building Council (NZGBC) 'Climate Scenarios for the Construction and Property Sector'. These were developed with participation from a wide range of industry and public sector stakeholders, and Fletcher Building contributed through representation on the NZGBC Leadership Group and Technical Working Group."
answers.notes: The NZGBC scenarios were developed with participation from a wide range of industry and public sector stakeholders. Fletcher Building itself participated in the development through the NZGBC Leadership Group and Technical Working Group, indicating co-production/participatory development.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: Yes. A baseline year of 2020 is used in the physical risk modelling. Additionally, the data sources reference a "Current Policy Reference Case" (aligned to NGFS Hot-House World / Current Policies) which functions as a reference scenario for some data variables. A 2020 baseline is explicitly used in the RMS Climate on Demand modelling time horizons.
answers.evidence: "Time horizons: 2020 (baseline), 2030, 2050, 2100." and from Appendix D: "Carbon price and oil price projections: He Pou a Rangi / Climate Change Commission (CCC), 2021 ... Tailwinds / Tailwinds & Headwinds / Current Policy Reference Case" and "Emissions trajectory data: Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) ... NGFS Net-Zero 2050 / NGFS Disorderly / NGFS Hot-House World"
answers.location_hint: Appendix E, p.75–76; Appendix D, p.73–74
answers.notes: The document uses 2020 as a baseline year for physical risk modelling time horizons. A "Current Policy Reference Case" is referenced in Appendix D as the scenario framing for the Hot House World scenario for some data variables. This is not explicitly described as a standalone "baseline scenario" against which the others are compared, but it functions as a reference point.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: "Using downscaled Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) climate projections and RMS Climate on Demand modelling ... Modelling tool: RMS Climate on Demand – uses downscaled CMIP6 projections adjusted to baseline hazard levels." and "Relative change in labour productivity due to heat stress in NZ: NGFS Climate Impact Explorer." and "Emissions trajectory data: Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS), International Institute for Applied Systems Analysis (IIASA) Scenario Explorer (2021)." and "Carbon price and oil price projections: He Pou a Rangi / Climate Change Commission (CCC), 2021: Scenarios dataset for Draft Advice for Consultation (ENZ model)." and "Combined Hazard Information Platform (CHIP) catastrophe risk-profiling tool: Aon"
answers.location_hint: Physical risk modelling section, p.21; Appendix D, p.73–74; Appendix E, p.75–76
answers.notes: RMS Climate on Demand is the primary physical risk modelling tool. CMIP6 provides the underlying climate projections. NGFS Climate Impact Explorer, IIASA Scenario Explorer, Aon CHIP, and the CCC ENZ model are used for specific data inputs.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: "During FY23, the impacts of Cyclone Gabrielle and the North Island floods in New Zealand were consistent with the levels of exposure and loss indicated by our historic scenario analysis for sites identified as being at high or very high flood risk. The impact amounted to $21 million for property damage and direct remedial works." and "Risk metric: Average Annual Loss (AAL), derived from: Annual Damage Rate (ADR) × Total Insured Value (TIV). Includes property damage, business interruption, insurance deductibles/premiums, higher operating costs." Hazards assessed include acute risks: Floods, Cyclones, Wildfires.
answers.location_hint: Physical Risks section, p.16–17; Appendix E, p.75–76
answers.notes: The document explicitly includes acute physical risks (floods, cyclones/hurricanes/typhoons, wildfires) in the scenario analysis and provides financial loss estimates (AAL metric). Historical actual losses from Cyclone Gabrielle ($21 million) are also disclosed. The document notes the AAL approach does not capture the acute impact of rare, severe events (e.g., a 1-in-200-year flood), but acute hazards are still modelled.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answer: New Zealand population and age distribution
answers.evidence: "DATA TYPE / VARIABLE ... Percentage increase in average number of hot days per year ... Increase in rainfall intensity data ... Increase in extreme wind speeds data ... Average sea-level rise (NZ) data ... Carbon price and oil price projections ... Electricity grid emissions ... Relative change in labour productivity due to heat stress in NZ ... Global GDP data ... Net carbon emissions forestry data ... Projected changes in carbon, fossil fuel use and energy efficiency for buildings ... New Zealand population and age distribution projections"
answers.location_hint: Appendix D, p.73–74; Appendix E, p.75–76
answers.notes: These variables are explicitly listed in Appendix D (data sources for the three scenarios) and Appendix E (hazard metrics table).

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: "The data for this scenario is sourced from downscaled NIWA projections from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change Fifth Assessment Report (IPCC AR5) ... Using downscaled Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) climate projections and RMS Climate on Demand modelling, we assessed risk exposures from heat stress, water stress, flooding, wildfires, hurricanes and sea-level rise across multiple climate scenarios and time horizons ... Uncertainty introduced through climate model downscaling and bias correction."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, p.13; Physical risk modelling section, p.21; Appendix E, p.76
answers.notes: Global temperature outcomes are drawn from global IPCC/SSP/RCP scenarios, but physical climate data is downscaled to New Zealand (via NIWA/MfE) and Australia (via BOM) regional levels. The RMS Climate on Demand tool uses downscaled CMIP6 projections for asset-level assessment.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Physical risk assessment: Short-term (Present–2030), Medium-term (2030–2050), Long-term (2050–2100). Transition risk assessment: Short-term (Present–2030), Medium-term (2030–2040), Long-term (Beyond 2040). Rationales are explicitly provided for all time horizons.
answers.evidence: "Physical risk assessment: These time horizons were selected because the construction and property sectors in general, and our operations specifically, hold long-lived assets that will be subject to the long-term impacts of climate change. They are also aligned with the Group's business planning and asset management processes. Short-term (Present – 2030): The short-term horizon aligns with the 5-year business planning cycle ... Medium-term (2030 – 2050): The medium-term horizon aligns with Fletcher Building's 'Net Zero by 2050' strategic goal. Long-term (2050 – 2100): The long-term horizon broadly aligns to a 50-year asset lifetime for significant physical assets. ... Transition risk assessment: ... the medium-term and long-term horizons for transition risk assessment are shorter than the physical risk assessment. This is because the nature of transition risks ... means that there is limited value (and very high uncertainty) in assessments beyond one to two business planning cycles (approximately 5 to 10 years)."
answers.notes: Rationales are explicitly provided for each time horizon for both physical and transition risk assessments. The physical risk modelling uses 2020 (baseline), 2030, 2050, and 2100 as specific data points.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: "This modelling is considered the most appropriate as it aligns with the approach our insurers use to assess physical risk exposure across our sites. As insurance cover for physical risks represents the Group's primary financial response to such events, having consistency between our internal risk assessment and insurer methodologies strengthens the link between risk identification and risk transfer, with a focus on sites most material to the business and business operations." And: "The 'Orderly' scenario is arguably no longer considered reasonable, given that achieving a below-1.5°C pathway is increasingly viewed as unlikely. However, that scenario is maintained for comparability with international frameworks, alignment with regulatory guidance, and to assess the [full range of outcomes]."
answers.location_hint: Physical risk modelling section, p.21; Strategy section, p.15
answers.notes: Justification is provided for: (1) the choice of RMS Climate on Demand modelling (alignment with insurer methodology); (2) retaining the Orderly scenario (comparability with international frameworks and regulatory guidance); (3) the NZGBC scenarios (developed specifically for the NZ construction and property sector with industry participation). The document also explains why SSP1-2.6 was not explicitly modelled (limitations in model availability at asset-level scale) and why climate analogues were used instead.
